subject: science
title: science_4.json
description: Grade 4 Science Assessment - Day 4: Electricity, Light, and Circuits

questions.q: What is required to make an electric current flow to light up a bulb?
options: An open circuit with a broken wire
A plastic switch in the off position
questions.hint: Electrical energy needs an unbroken loop to travel from the source and back.
questions.explanation: Electricity requires a continuous, closed path (circuit) made of conducting materials for the electrons to flow.

questions.q: Which material is an excellent conductor of electricity?
questions.hint: Most electrical cords have this metal hidden inside them.
questions.explanation: Metals like copper allow electrical energy to pass through them easily, making them excellent conductors.

questions.q: What term describes a material that does not allow electricity to pass through it easily?
questions.hint: Rubber, plastic, and wood are examples of this type of material.
questions.explanation: An insulator prevents or highly resists the flow of electrical current, protecting us from shocks.

questions.q: If you remove one lightbulb from a series circuit containing three bulbs, what happens to the remaining bulbs?
options: The other two bulbs go out completely.
The other two bulbs burn twice as bright.
Only the bulb closest to the battery stays lit.
The remaining bulbs start blinking rapidly.
questions.answer: The other two bulbs go out completely.
questions.hint: In this specific type of circuit, all components are connected in a single, continuous loop.
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for electricity. Removing a bulb breaks the path, creating an open circuit that stops the current entirely.

questions.q: How does light travel from a light source like a flashlight?
options: In curved lines that bend around objects
In random, zig-zag patterns across the room
Only in upward circles toward the ceiling
questions.hint: Think about how shadows are sharp and clear when you block a beam.
questions.explanation: Light energy travels outward from its source in straight lines known as light rays until it strikes an object.

questions.q: What behavior of light is shown when a mirror bounces light rays back into a room?
questions.hint: This word describes what you see when you look at yourself in a calm lake.
questions.explanation: Reflection occurs when light waves bounce off a smooth, shiny surface like a mirror.

questions.q: Why does a straw placed in a clear glass of water look bent or broken at the surface?
options: Light waves bend as they change speed moving from air to water.
The water dissolves the structural strength of plastic straws.
The glass magnifies the top half while shrinking the bottom half.
Light is completely absorbed by the water molecules.
questions.answer: Light waves bend as they change speed moving from air to water.
questions.hint: This optical illusion is caused by the refraction of light.
questions.explanation: Refraction is the bending of light as it passes from one medium (like air) into another medium of a different density (like water), which alters its speed.

questions.q: An object that allows some light to pass through but scatters it so you cannot see clearly through it is:
questions.hint: Frosted glass windows or wax paper are good examples.
questions.explanation: Translucent materials allow light to pass through imperfectly, scattering the rays so objects on the other side appear blurry.

questions.q: Which type of material completely blocks light from passing through, creating a dark shadow behind it?
questions.hint: A brick wall, a wooden door, or your own body are examples.
questions.explanation: Opaque materials absorb or reflect all incident light, preventing any light from passing through them.

questions.q: What transparent, triangular glass tool can separate white light into the full spectrum of colors?
questions.hint: It creates a mini-rainbow by bending different colors of light at slightly different angles.
questions.explanation: A prism refracts white light, separating it into its component wavelengths to reveal the rainbow colors of the visible spectrum.
